design_philosophy.core_loop: Select a monster, climb buildings, destroy them by punching, eat humans and food for health recovery, advance to next city after destroying all structures
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Managing monster health while destroying buildings under fire from military forces (helicopters, tanks, soldiers); balancing aggression with survival
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as a giant destructive monster (King Kong-like gorilla, Godzilla-like reptile, or werewolf) demolishing cities and consuming everything in your path
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Designer Brian Colin explicitly designed it with 'no wrong way to play' - rejecting common arcade conventions of fixed objectives, high score competition, and death penalties. The game is fundamentally about destruction and freedom rather than precision or scoring

difficulty.lives_system: Monsters revert to human form when health depleted; humans walk off screen (lost permanently unless continuing). Continue system allows re-entry via blimp with full health but lost score
difficulty.continues_system: Players can continue after monsters revert to human form; continuing monster re-enters on blimp with full health bar but score reset
difficulty.checkpointing: No explicit checkpointing; progression is level-based (128 days across North American cities). Each day/level must be completed by destroying all buildings
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player choice of three monsters (George, Lizzie, Ralph) with presumably different abilities; resource management through selective eating of food items vs. harmful items (bombs, electrical appliances); optional score pursuit vs. simple survival

progression.upgrade_system: No traditional upgrades; monster health recovers through eating food items found in buildings (fruit, roast chicken, soldiers). Different items have different health values and risks
progression.unlock_mechanism: Sequential city progression across 128 days (Peoria, Illinois to Plano, Illinois). Cycle repeats 5 times before reset. Hidden day select cheat (B+Select on Controller 1 and B on Controller 2) allows jumping to any of 99 accessible days
replayability_hooks: Three distinct playable monsters (George, Lizzie, Ralph) with different forms/abilities
Multiple cities with varied layouts across 128 days
Score-chasing for those interested in high score competition
Variable item spawning in windows creates unpredictability
Two-player multiplayer option (monsters can punch each other)

world_building_techniques: Opening cutscene explaining mutation origins (experimental vitamins, radioactive waste, food additive)
Three distinct monster designs representing different origin stories (King Kong inspiration for George, Godzilla for Lizzie, werewolf for Ralph)
Geographic progression through North American cities (Peoria to Plano, spanning 50 US states plus Canadian cities)
Military opposition and civilian NPCs provide implicit narrative context of monster threat to civilization
Post-stage text screens (with some placeholder/unused text) provide context between levels

faction_indicators: Player monsters vs. military forces (helicopters, tanks, soldiers, police cars)
Civilians fleeing (NPCs waving from windows signaling for help)
Three-player cooperative or competitive monster dynamic
danger_signals: Hazardous building items (bombs, electrical appliances, photographers with flash)
Environmental damage (falling, punches from other monsters)
environmental_storytelling: High-rise city architecture represents human civilization targets
Presence of military vehicles and personnel indicate organized response to monster threat
Civilians in windows represent unprotected population
Food items (roast chicken, soldiers) reflect consumption/survival mechanic
Repeated city cycles suggest ongoing rampage rather than one-time event

sources_used: tcrf - The Cutting Room Floor (Rampage NES page): developer, hidden features, unused content
wikipedia - Rampage (video game): core gameplay mechanics, monster origins, design philosophy quotes, progression structure, narrative context
